questions.questionText: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is based on the principle that:
questions.optionB: Dysfunctional thoughts lead to maladaptive behaviors and emotions, and changing these cognitive distortions can improve mental health
questions.optionC: Past childhood experiences determine current behavior
questions.optionD: Behavior is entirely determined by reinforcement
questions.explanation: Core concept: CBT targets cognitive distortions → changes thoughts → improves emotions and behaviors. Why correct: CBT (Aaron Beck): identifies and challenges negative automatic thoughts (cognitive distortions — all-or-nothing thinking, catastrophizing, mind-reading, overgeneralization). Restructuring thoughts → changes emotions and behaviors. Time-limited, structured, evidence-based. Why A wrong: Psychoanalysis (Freud) — unconscious motivations, free association, transference. Why C wrong: Psychodynamic therapy — past experiences shaping current behavior. Why D wrong: Behaviorism (Skinner) — operant conditioning, no cognitive component. Clinical relevance: CBT is first-line psychotherapy for: depression, anxiety disorders, OCD, PTSD, eating disorders, insomnia (CBT-I). Evidence base strongest of all psychotherapies. Exam trap: CBT = evidence-based for most psychiatric conditions. DBT (Dialectical BT) = specifically for BPD. Know the difference. Memory aid: 'CBT = Change Bad Thinking (to improve feelings and actions).' Reference: Ahuja – Psychotherapy.
